fact: September 6 is exactly 249 days into the year, which means there are only 116 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and that's why you suddenly see them everywhere indoors!
The sun is setting about 2-3 minutes earlier each day around September 6, making the evenings noticeably shorter than just a week ago.
September 6 falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, when people born under this zodiac sign are supposedly extra organized and picky about everything.
Monarch butterflies in North America are in full migration mode right now, with some flying over 2,000 miles to reach their winter homes in Mexico!
In many northern regions, the first maple leaves start showing tiny hints of red and orange around this date, even though summer isn't officially over.
September 6 is roughly 2 weeks before the autumn equinox, when day and night become exactly equal length everywhere on Earth.
Squirrels are in peak hoarding mode around this time, burying up to 10,000 nuts and seeds to prepare for winter - and they forget where they put about half of them!
The morning dew gets noticeably heavier around September 6 in temperate climates because the temperature difference between day and night is growing larger.
In the Southern Hemisphere, September 6 marks the heart of late winter, when many animals like wombats and echidnas start becoming more active as spring approaches.
Cricket chirping reaches its slowest pace of the year around this date in cooler regions - you can actually estimate the temperature by counting their chirps!
